avalancheActivity.comment: Large amounts of fresh snow and drifts from last week blanket a weak old snowpack. Avalanches can easily be triggered by one single winter sports enthusiast in all aspects, especially in little-skied terrain. Danger zones increase with ascending altitude, occur widespread in terrain near to and distant from ridgelines. Caution urged in transitions from shallow to deep snow, for example, at entries into gullies and bowls. Remote triggerings are possible. Avalanches can be large-sized and plummet over flattish passages. As a result of renewed rainfall and the snowpack becoming ever wetter, wet slab avalanches can be expected at low and intermediate altitudes. If these fracture to deeper down layers of the snowpack, they can in isolated cases grow to large size. On steep grass-covered slopes, glide-snow avalanches can trigger. Zones with glide cracks should under all circumstances be avoided.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Dangerous avalanche situation persists apart from heavily skied freerider terrain

snowpackStructure.comment: By midday up to 15cm of fresh snow is expected above 2000m, accompanied by strong-velocity winds; below that altitude, rainfall expected. The large amounts of fresh snow from the last few days have been repeatedly transported in varying directions, snowdrift accumulations are poorly bonded with the old snowpack. Weak layers are widely prevalent in the lower part of the snowpack. The extraordinary avalanche activity last week confirms just how prone to triggering the weak layer is. This layer persists apart from heavily skied zones and in starting zones which have not yet discharged. At low and intermediate altitudes the snowpack is thoroughly wet. Even at higher altitudes there are breakable crusts, particularly in areas exposed to the sun.

tendency.highlights: Avalanche danger is gradually diminishing, but the overall situation for winter sports enthusiasts remains extremely treacherous.

weatherForecast.comment: Rainfall at night and until midday, snowfall above 1800m. During the afternoon hours, clouds will disperse somewhat. Temperature at 2000m: -3 to +1 degree, at 3000m at -5 degrees. Initially strong to storm-strength northwesterly winds at high altitudes will slacken off significantly during the afternoon..

avalancheActivity.comment: Avalanches can be triggered by one single winter sports enthusiast. Danger zones occur on steep slopes in all aspects, behind drops in the landscape. Particularly on very steep shady slopes above 1800m, avalanches can fracture down to deeper-down layers inside the snowpack and then grow to large size.  Whumpf noises and fractures which appear when treading upon the snowpack are alarm signals. At low and intermediate altitudes, medium-sized wet loose-snow avalanches can be expected in very steep terrain. On steep grass-covered slopes, glide-snow avalanches can occur. Zones with glide cracks should under all circumstances be avoided.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Danger of slab avalanches at high altitudes, wet loose-snow avalanches at lower avalanches

snowpackStructure.comment: Only a small amount of fresh snowfall is anticipated, accompanied by strong-velocity winds. Bonding of snowdrift accumulations generated last week in all aspects to the generally weak snowpack is moderate to weak. On shady slopes there are weak layers in the lower part of the snowpack. The snowpack is heavy all the way to the ridgelines and has melt-freeze crusts widespread.

tendency.highlights: The situation for winter sports enthusiasts persists.

weatherForecast.comment: On Monday, gray and foggy skies, only brief bright intervals. Light intermittent snowfall, snowfall level at about 1600m. Temperature at 2000m: rising from -1 degree, at 3000m at -6 degrees. Strong to storm-strength northwesterly winds at high altitudes.

ALBINA.generalHeadline: Dangerous avalanche situation persists apart from heavily skied freerider terrain
